name: Difference between interface and abstract class (Java 8+)
question_text: In Java 8 and later, which statement about interfaces versus abstract classes is correct?
general_feedback: A class can implement many interfaces but extend only one class (abstract or concrete). Abstract classes can hold mutable instance state and non-public constructors; interfaces cannot hold mutable instance fields (only public static final constants) and have no constructors. Both can now define concrete methods (via default/static methods on interfaces, regular methods on abstract classes).
correct_feedback: Correct — Java supports multiple interface inheritance but only single class inheritance.

answers.text: A class may implement multiple interfaces but may extend only one (abstract) class.
answers.feedback: Java supports multiple interface inheritance but only single class inheritance.

answers.text: An interface can hold mutable instance fields just like an abstract class.
answers.feedback: Interface fields are implicitly public static final — not mutable per-instance.
Abstract classes can define constructors, invoked by subclasses via super(...).

answers.text: Interfaces cannot have any method bodies at all.
answers.feedback: Since Java 8, interfaces allow default and static methods with bodies.
